request.question: Which of the following directed "House on Haunted Hill": John Schwert or William Malone?

title: House on Haunted Hill (1999 film)
Return to House on Haunted Hill
Supernova (2000 film)
Parasomnia (film)
John Schwert
House on Haunted Hill
The Plagues
William Malone (director)
The House at Haunted Hill
Thirteen Ghosts
sentences: House on Haunted Hill is a 1999 American horror film directed by William Malone and starring Geoffrey Rush, Famke Janssen, Taye Diggs, Ali Larter, and Jeffrey Combs.
It also includes a cameo appearance by Peter Graves.
The plot follows a group of strangers who are invited to a party at an abandoned asylum, where they are offered $1 million each by an amusement park mogul if they are able to survive the night.

Produced by Robert Zemeckis and Joel Silver, it is a remake of the 1959 film of the same title directed by William Castle, and features special effects by famed make-up artists Gregory Nicotero and Dick Smith.
Return to House on Haunted Hill is a 2007 direct-to-DVD horror film and the sequel to the 1999 film "House on Haunted Hill".
Directed by Víctor García and written by William Masa, the film stars Amanda Righetti, Tom Riley, Cerina Vincent and Erik Palladino.

The film follows Ariel Wolfe - younger sister of Sara Wolfe; the previous films protagonist - being forced by a group of people to search for a mysterious idol hidden inside a haunted psychiatric asylum.
Supernova is a 2000 Swiss-American science fiction horror film written by David C. Wilson, William Malone and Daniel Chuba and directed by Walter Hill, credited as "Thomas Lee."
"Thomas Lee" was chosen as a directorial pseudonym for release, as the name Alan Smithee had become too well known as a badge of a film being disowned by its makers.
It was originally developed in 1988 by director William Malone as "Dead Star," with paintings by H. R. Giger and a plot that had been called ""Hellraiser" in outer space."
Jack Sholder was hired for substantial uncredited reshoots, and Francis Ford Coppola was brought in for editing purposes.
Various sources suggest that little of Hill's work remains in the theatrical cut of the film.
The film shares several plot similarities with the film "Event Horizon," released in 1997, and "Alien Cargo," released in 1999.
The cast featured James Spader, Angela Bassett, Robert Forster, Lou Diamond Phillips, Peter Facinelli, Robin Tunney, and Wilson Cruz.
This film was shot by cinematographer Lloyd Ahern and scored by composers David C. Williams and Burkhard Dallwitz.
Parasomnia is an independent horror film directed by William Malone and stars Jeffrey Combs, Timothy Bottoms and Dylan Purcell.
The filming was funded by Malone himself, and its release was delayed due to difficulties securing distribution.
John Schwert (born November 20, 1975) is a film and commercial director and producer born in Fredonia, New York and owner/operator of "Fourth Ward Productions".
He is represented by "Trailer Park Films" for Canadian commercials and content/media creation.
John Schwert is related to Pius L. Schwert.
House on Haunted Hill is a 1959 American horror film directed by William Castle.

The film was written by Robb White and stars Vincent Price and Carol Ohmart as eccentric millionaire Frederick Loren and his wife Annabelle, who have invited five people to the house for a "haunted house" party.
Whoever stays in the house for one night will earn $10,000.
As the night progresses, all the guests are trapped inside the house with ghosts, murderers, and other terrors.
The Plagues were an American garage rock band from Lansing, Michigan who were active in the 1960s (not to be confused with other bands of the period named "the Plagues").
They were led by bass player, principal songwriter, and vocalist William Malone.
The group had a local and regional hit with "I’ve Been Through It Before".
Malone left the band in 1966 to join the Frightened Trees, as the remaining members formed a new group out of the ashes of the Plagues, the Plain Brown Wrapper.

Malone later became a successful Hollywood movie director, whose 2008 horror thriller "Parasomnia" included "I’ve Been Through It Before", as well as other songs by the Plagues and other 1960s garage bands.
In the intervening years the Plagues work has attracted a following amongst garage rock enthusiasts and collectors.
William Malone (born 1953) is an American horror filmmaker who directed such films as the 1999 remake "House on Haunted Hill", "Scared to Death", "Creature", and "FeardotCom".
The House at Haunted Hill is a high-tech haunted attraction at a home in Woodland Hills, California.
The attraction, technically a yard haunt, showcases a mixture of age-old tricks such as Pepper's ghost, and advanced special effects and multi-channel audio.
Thirteen Ghosts (also known as 13 Ghosts and stylized as THIR13EN Ghosts) is a 2001 Canadian-American supernatural horror film directed by Steve Beck.
It is a remake of the 1960 film "13 Ghosts" by William Castle.
It follows the remake of another one of Castle's films, "House on Haunted Hill", and was shot entirely around Lower Mainland, British Columbia.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

hop1_docs: William Malone (director) | William Malone (born 1953) is an American horror filmmaker who directed such films as the 1999 remake "House on Haunted Hill", "Scared to Death", "Creature", and "FeardotCom".

House on Haunted Hill (1999 film) | House on Haunted Hill is a 1999 American horror film directed by William Malone and starring Geoffrey Rush, Famke Janssen, Taye Diggs, Ali Larter, and Jeffrey Combs.  It also includes a cameo appearance by Peter Graves.  The plot follows a group of strangers who are invited to a party at an abandoned asylum, where they are offered $1 million each by an amusement park mogul if they are able to survive the night.  Produced by Robert Zemeckis and Joel Silver, it is a remake of the 1959 film of the same title directed by William Castle, and features special effects by famed make-up artists Gregory Nicotero and Dick Smith.

Return to House on Haunted Hill | Return to House on Haunted Hill is a 2007 direct-to-DVD horror film and the sequel to the 1999 film "House on Haunted Hill".  Directed by Víctor García and written by William Masa, the film stars Amanda Righetti, Tom Riley, Cerina Vincent and Erik Palladino.  The film follows Ariel Wolfe - younger sister of Sara Wolfe; the previous films protagonist - being forced by a group of people to search for a mysterious idol hidden inside a haunted psychiatric asylum.

House on Haunted Hill | House on Haunted Hill is a 1959 American horror film directed by William Castle.  The film was written by Robb White and stars Vincent Price and Carol Ohmart as eccentric millionaire Frederick Loren and his wife Annabelle, who have invited five people to the house for a "haunted house" party.  Whoever stays in the house for one night will earn $10,000.  As the night progresses, all the guests are trapped inside the house with ghosts, murderers, and other terrors.

Hillbillys in a Haunted House | Hillbillys in a Haunted House is a 1967 American musical horror comedy film starring Ferlin Husky and Joi Lansing, and directed by Jean Yarbrough.  The film is a sequel to "The Las Vegas Hillbillys" (1966), with Joi Lansing replacing Mamie Van Doren in the role of "Boots Malone".

Thirteen Ghosts | Thirteen Ghosts (also known as 13 Ghosts and stylized as THIR13EN Ghosts) is a 2001 Canadian-American supernatural horror film directed by Steve Beck.  It is a remake of the 1960 film "13 Ghosts" by William Castle.  It follows the remake of another one of Castle's films, "House on Haunted Hill", and was shot entirely around Lower Mainland, British Columbia.

Hell House (film) | Hell House is a 2001 documentary that was directed by George Ratliff that focuses on the idea of a hell house, a Christian-themed haunted attraction, in Cedar Hill, Texas.  The film follows the youth group of the Cedar Hill Trinity Church, documenting the work involved in creating the hell house, the performances themselves, and the personal lives of some of the participants.

response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- William Malone — [1]: "directed such films as the 1999 remake \"House on Haunted Hill\","
- House on Haunted Hill (1999 film) — [2]: "House on Haunted Hill is a 1999 American horror film directed by William Malone"
- House on Haunted Hill (1959 film) — [4]: "House on Haunted Hill is a 1959 American horror film directed by William Castle."

Ambiguities:
- "House on Haunted Hill" corresponds to multiple films: 1999 (William Malone) and 1959 (William Castle).
- John Schwert — not mentioned in the provided passages (no evidence present for this name).

Bridge / missing fact:
- The passages do not mention John Schwert or attribute any "House on Haunted Hill" film to him. The only direct attribution in the passages is that William Malone directed the 1999 remake. Final conclusion: William Malone directed "House on Haunted Hill" (the 1999 film).

Retrieval direction:
- Fetch page: "John Schwert" or run query: "John Schwert House on Haunted Hill"
- Fetch page: "House on Haunted Hill (disambiguation)"
- (Optional verify) Fetch page: "William Malone (director)" and "House on Haunted Hill (1999 film)"
- No further retrieval required to answer between the two choices above; final answer: William Malone.
hop2_docs: John Schwert | John Schwert (born November 20, 1975) is a film and commercial director and producer born in Fredonia, New York and owner/operator of "Fourth Ward Productions".  He is represented by "Trailer Park Films" for Canadian commercials and content/media creation.  John Schwert is related to Pius L. Schwert.

William Malone (director) | William Malone (born 1953) is an American horror filmmaker who directed such films as the 1999 remake "House on Haunted Hill", "Scared to Death", "Creature", and "FeardotCom".
John Malone (disambiguation) | John Malone is a businessman and philanthropist.
William Malone Baskervill | William Malone Baskervill (1850–1899) was a writer and professor of the English language and literature at Vanderbilt University.

James William Malone | James William Malone (March 8, 1920—April 9, 2000) was a 20th-century American prelate of the Catholic Church.  He served as Bishop of Youngstown in the state of Ohio from 1968-1995.

The Amazing Mr. Malone | The Amazing Mr. Malone (also known as Murder and Mr. Malone) is an American radio crime drama series based on the John Malone series of mystery novels by Craig Rice.  The series began on ABC in 1948 and was broadcast on NBC Radio in 1951.
Steve Malone | Steven John Malone (born 1953) is a former English cricketer.  Malone was a right-handed batsman who bowled right-arm fast-medium.
response.summary_2: William Malone directed the 1999 remake "House on Haunted Hill"; the provided passages give no evidence that John Schwert directed that film.
